section_title: INTRODUCTION TO ICT PRICE ACTION MODEL 6: BUYSIDE TRADE PLAN
content:
Welcome back. This is the Price Action Model number six universal trade plan, focusing primarily on the
buy side of the market maker models.
- ICT price action model number six universal trade plan: buy side of the market maker models.

Every trade plan video has an overview. In each trading plan, we look for five stages:
- Preparation
- Opportunity Discovery
- Trade Planning
- Trade Execution
- Trade Management

- ECONOMIC EVENTS AND MARKET STRUCTURE
For Preparation, we're going to note all medium and high impact events for the markets that you're
following. We're going to study the events on the week to come and consider how the current market
structure and the calendar events may suggest a specific weekly profile for that week's range.

- IPA DATA RANGES AND DEALING RANGE
We're going to determine the IPA data range for the last 20, 40, and 60 days. We do not count Sundays
and note the highest high and the lowest low in the past 20 trading days. This is your current dealing range.
The reason why we're going to be looking at the 20, 40, and 60 is because this is a universal trade model,
so it's allowing us to trade in multiple time frames.

- TIMEFRAME SPECIFIC LOOKBACKS
This begins with a 20-day look back, and then 40-day, and then 60-day, depending upon what time frame
you're using. So if you're using like a weekly setup, or if you're using a daily setup, or 4-hour, 1-hour, that
type of framework, you're going to look at the longer term for a weekly chart. In other words, we'll look at
the 60-day look back, and that will be attributed to weekly setups. Daily setups can be 40 to 20 and
sometimes 60 days, depending upon if you've gone outside the range. The point is, you're going to define
the last 20, 40, and 60 trading days, and you're going to determine that range. The highest high and the
lowest low, splitting that range in half, gives us our premium and discount PD array Matrix. So we're going
to be looking for primarily buying in discount ranges.

- LIQUIDITY AND MARKET MAKER MODELS
Inside this dealing range, we're going to look for the next draw on liquidity. In this case, where is price likely
to trade to next, above which old high? We look for our premium PD array in the direction of our weekly
range bias. Now you understand enough about the market maker buy and sell models to know that when
we're looking at the market maker buy model, the original consolidation, we start near the low or inside of a
discount market. The market will trade up to a premium, reverse, and trade lower and go below the original
consolidation. In a market maker buy model, it starts at a premium consolidation, it declines down to a
discount where it reverses and trades back above the original consolidation. So we're going to be looking
for that premise on the time frame that we're studying for our setups.

- VOLATILITY INJECTION
We're going to anticipate price to move to a premium PD array that would support our weekly bias on a day
and economic event found on the economic calendar with the current or next trading week. This volatility
injection is what we wait for. This will be a run on the basis of a low resistance liquidity run.

- MENTAL FRAMEWORK
The image to the left is your mental framework on how the trade should pan out. This is an example of a
market maker sell model where it goes up first to drop down.

- IDENTIFYING PREMIUM PD ARRAYS FOR SELL
MODELS
You're going to identify a premium PD array above the market. In this case, it may be a bearish order block
or a bearish breaker that you expect the market to trade up to and then react to going lower. So the
framework on this is directly linked to your understanding on how the market's going to swing higher to
make a sell opportunity, not just, 'okay, it's to sell right now because I'm looking at the marketplace.' You
need to be able to forecast that the market is at present market conditions and market pricing. You expect
the market to rally up once it goes up to a specific premium array. Again, it may be a bearish breaker,
bearish order block, it might be running an old high for like a turtle soup and then sell off. So it's not like just
waiting for it to keep going up in perpetuity. You're waiting for it to go up and in that movement from current
market action and pricing up to that premium array. In other words, think of it as we're in an oversold
market, okay, and we expect it to trade up to an overbought condition into a resistance level. Now, they are
retail terms, but we look for things that are narratively driven. And we're going to look for stage one or stage
two of reaccumulation to go long and target the Terminus at the premium PD. Now, Terminus is where you
think the market's going to go up to, stop, and turn around and go the other direction. So it's important to
understand that we're not getting in this trade with the idea that it's going to explode and become a mega
trade and go, you know, three, four, five months in one direction. We're looking for a very scalable,
forecasted move that sends price higher, but generally up to a point in which it creates a sell. So in this
trade plan, we're focusing primarily on the buy side of the curve of both the buy and sell models.

- IDENTIFYING PREMIUM PD ARRAYS FOR BUY
MODELS
And the reverse is seen when we have a market maker buy model. We're going to identify a premium PD
array, old high, or in this case, the original consolidation above the market that price will likely reach up to
to complete a market maker buy model. Again, we're going to wait for stage one and or stage two
reaccumulation period to go long and target the Terminus at the premium PD array. In this case, because
it's the market maker buy model, we're going to be targeting the premium array in the form of old high or
relative equal highs that would describe the original consolidation prior to the drop down and then reversal.
And when it starts to go up, that's the buy side of the curve of a market maker buy model. We're focusing
primarily on that. So you wouldn't be really trading with this plan as it goes short. You're just waiting for it to
trade down to that discount array, create a market structure break to the upside, and then you would start
looking for regions to be long in stage one or two reaccumulation.

- ENTRY CONVERGENCE AND VOLATILITY
When the market is primed to rally higher, we want to look for a convergence of both manipulation and
price opposite to our trade bias. In this case, something dropping lower for a Judas swing at a time that the
economic calendar suggests a volatility injection will likely unfold. We will buy discount fair value gaps or
buy sell stops in a stage one or two reaccumulation.

- LONG ENTRY CONDITIONS AND SCALPING PROTOCOLS
When we are bullish, we will frame a long entry when price has moved down into a 15 or five minute
discount fair value Gap PD that converges with a standard deviation of no more than -3 standard deviations
during London open or New York open. We can implement scalping protocols on this stage as well for
further reduction in risk.

- TARGETING TERMINUS BASED ON MARKET MAKER
MODEL
When we are in a market maker sell model, we will target the buy side liquidity above the old high as
Terminus. If we are in a market maker buy model, we will target the original consolidation of the market
maker buy model.

- INSTITUTIONAL ORDER FLOW ENTRY
When we are bullish, we will anticipate a 5-minute chart institutional order flow entry drill trade entry to form
inside of a retracement lower during London open and or New York open kill zones or a sell stop raid that
will go long when it unfolds.

- BUY LIMIT ORDER PLACEMENT
When we are entering a long, we will place a buy limit order on all positions. We will execute with our demo
account. We will use the standard deviation and PD array convergence plus 5 Pips as our entry price when
using the buy limit order. If multiple orders are used, all use the same entry price in the buy limit order.

- PROFIT OBJECTIVES
When we're entering a long, we will place a limit order to take 20 Pips as our objective on one position. We
will place a second limit order to take 40 Pips as our second objective. We will use multiple orders to
manage the trade idea. If you capture 60 pip objective, and this is assuming you have at least three
positions on or three trade entries, separate orders, you're going to close 80% of the trade and see if it has
more room to run.

- STOP LOSS PLACEMENT AND RE-ENTRY
When we're entering a long, we will note the discount array and standard deviation convergence we aim to
enter at. We will place our stop loss below this low minus 20 Pips. We will re-enter if the trade stops out.
We can monitor it for secondary entry. Day trades may require multiple attempts to secure a solid entry. Do
not fear that.

- PROGRESSIVE STOP LOSS REDUCTION
When we earn profit 25% of our expected objective, stop loss can be reduced by 25%. When we are in
profit 50% of our expected objective, stop loss can be reduced by 50%. When the position is at 75% of the
expected profit objective, stop must be at break even. Now, when you look at this, it seems like you're
holding a lot of open risk, but remember, we're taking something off at 20 Pips, we're taking something off
at 40 Pips, and something at 60. 80% is coming off, and in the remainder, there isn't much risk remaining in
the trade because we're banking as we go further in progression.

- POSITION SIZE CALCULATION FORMULA
Money management, and obviously these slides don't change. Position size calculation formula is: Position
size equals Account Equity multiplied by Risk Percent divided by Stop Loss in Pips.
- Position size is the amount of leverage or trade or trades assumed.
- Account Equity is the total amount in your trading account.
- Risk Percent is the percent of risk you are willing to take on per trade. Now, if you're using multiple orders,
you have to divide that in the total number of orders you're going to have. So if you're willing to risk 2% and
you're going to be using four orders, let's say, so four separate orders at one half of 1% would give us the
same thing as 2% risk.
- The difference between the entry price and your stop loss is the number of Pips you will use to divide the
result of equity times Risk Percent.

As an example, Equity of $20,000, risk per trade is 1.5% or $20,000 multiplied by 1.5%. In this case, it
would be $300. Stop required for the trade is 20 Pips.
- Micro Lots: 1,000 each or 10 cents per pip. 20 Pips times 10 cents equals $2 USD. So $300 divided by $2
equals 150 Micro Lots per trade or 1.5% of the account Equity. Always round down.
- Mini Lots: Each mini lot is 10,000 each in leverage or $1 per pip. 20 Pips at $1 per pip equals $20 USD.
So $300 divided by $20 for a 20 pip stop loss at $1 per pip equals the same thing as 15 mini lots per trade
or 1.5% of the $20,000 Equity. Again, always round down. Mini lots are always more flexible.
- Standard Lot: Would be $100,000 in leverage or $10 per pip. Using a 20 pip stop loss in this trade plan at
$10 per pip would be a risk of $200. And with $300, which would be a maximum of 1.5% of $20,000, would
be $300 divided by 200, and that would be 1.5 standard lot or in this case, one lot per trade because you
can't break a standard lot up. That's why we like to use minis.

- RISK ADJUSTMENT PROTOCOLS
If your demo account takes a loss on a trade and is at a full R percent that you assumed, you're going to
drop your R percent by 50%. And when the loss is recovered by 50%, you're permitted to return to the
maximum R percent per trade. This is to say that if you lost $100, okay, you cannot trade the same amount
of leverage that you used when taking that $100 loss until you make $50 back in new profit, and then you
can round back up to the same leverage you were using before you took the $100 loss. If you reduce the R
percent trade and it assumes a loss as well, you reduce the R percent by 50% again until the previous
trade loss is recovered by 50%. If you take a series of five winning trades in a row, drop your R percent by
50%. You're likely to assume a loss eventually, and this will build in equity leveling and reduce the likelihood
of a large drawdown. You want a smooth equity curve that slopes or stair steps higher, not a jaggy roller
coaster with deep declines.
CONCLUSION:

Alright, so now you're going to start backtesting. You're going to collect multiple sample sets with this trade
plan. If you're unclear about some of the process, rewatch the lessons in this price action model. Dig into
your charts and study what was provided here. Until next time, I wish you good luck and good trading.
